header.headline: The compute-to-bandwidth chasm
header.subtitle: The board compares a bridgeable CPU terrain to a much harsher GPU-at-scale terrain without changing the family grammar.

legend.title: Comparison lanes
legend.note: The family compares system regimes while the payload carries the actual engineering claim.

left.title: Gentle terrain
left.subtitle: Peak compute, local memory, and network bandwidth stay close enough that software can bridge the gap.
bullets: Software can overlap compute and communication
left.note: The board should show that the left lane is a solvable engineering regime.

center.title: Everest to trench
center.subtitle: Peak compute rises far above the same network floor, leaving a gap software cannot fully erase.
bullets: The network floor does not rise with compute
Intra-node links help but do not remove the chasm
The starved-compute story is the central contrast
center.note: The center lane must still read as the same board, not as a different infographic family.

right.title: Bridge what can be bridged
right.subtitle: C-Kernel-Engine targets the regime where software can synchronize compute and data movement instead of assuming the network disappears.
bullets: Do not plan around missing bandwidth
Tune where software still has leverage
Use the board to compare regimes, not to hide constraints
right.note: The right lane turns the comparison into an action rule.

metric_1.label: Gap size
metric_1.detail: CPU and GPU-at-scale sit in different regimes

metric_2.label: Shared floor
metric_2.detail: Cross-node movement remains the slowest layer

callout_1.title: Software can close a gentle gap
callout_1.body: When compute and data movement live in the same neighborhood, engineering work can materially change the result.

callout_2.title: Do not assume the network disappears
callout_2.body: A useful comparison board makes the slowest layer visible so system design stays honest.

footer.note: The comparison board should stay generic enough to compare performance regimes, system stacks, or model choices without embedding topic-specific ids in the DSL.
